news.source: ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation

sections.heading: No changes confirmed, defence is the fix
sections.body: No fresh selection news has been confirmed as of writing. What is certain sits in the log: 39 points shipped in round one is the number the group has spent the week living with, and conversations in camp have reportedly centred on tightening the shape that let Wales in, rather than wholesale changes.

sections.heading: Nothing new since yesterday
sections.body: No fresh injury concerns have been confirmed ahead of Saturday's Test, and camp appears settled heading into England. That is the honest state of play right now — a late change to the matchday squad would be the first update of the week, not a continuation of an existing list.

sections.heading: England's XV still under wraps
sections.body: England's team for Saturday was not confirmed at the time of writing. What Fiji do know is the shape of the challenge: a heavier, more structured pack than Wales offered, built to slow ball and grind territory. The collision area — where round one already leaked 39 points — is where England will look to make Saturday count.

sections.heading: Fiji into the weekend at 10th, margin the story
sections.body: Fiji go into the weekend 10th of 12, PF 24, PA 39, PD -15, after the round one loss to Wales. Beating England on Saturday would put points on the board and reset the mood; lose again and the group needs results from the games in hand just to get back level.

sections.heading: England chase tone, not personnel, after South Africa defeat
sections.body: One result won't define this campaign, but a heavy defeat to South Africa in the opener leaves plenty to fix. The bigger story this week isn't a fresh name on the team sheet — it's tone: fewer errors under pressure, a tighter kicking game, and the discipline to stay in the fight deep into the second half against Fiji.

sections.heading: Clean bill of health for the Fiji trip
sections.body: No fresh fitness concerns have surfaced since Thursday, and no injury news has been filed against Saturday's Test in Fiji. England's touring group remains as selected, unchanged through the week. After a bruising, error-strewn opening Test, that continuity is a genuine mercy — no withdrawals, no late swaps, just the same group preparing to put things right.

sections.heading: Fiji's team news still to land
sections.body: Fiji hadn't confirmed their matchday 23 as of Friday, so the exact shape of Saturday's threat is still filling in. What's not in doubt is the identity: quick ball, offloads through contact, and a back three comfortable playing from deep inside their own 22. England's discipline at the breakdown will be tested from the first whistle.

sections.heading: Bottom of the log, one game to change it
sections.body: England go into the weekend bottom of the log — played one, won none, drawn none, lost one, PD -24 — after the 21-45 defeat to South Africa. There's no cushion to lean on; only a win in Fiji starts repairing that deficit. Round two already looks like a must-not-lose fixture this early in the campaign.

sections.heading: Response, not revolution
sections.body: Staff have kept this week's message simple: composure, not panic, after the New Zealand collapse. The talk out of camp is about a calmer start and sharper game management in Australia, not a rebuild. Expect the core group backed again, with last week's disappointment used as fuel rather than an excuse heading into a demanding away test.

sections.heading: Clean bill for Saturday's Test
sections.body: No withdrawals have been added to the group since Thursday, and the touring squad remains as named for Saturday's Test. It is the kind of quiet week France will take after last weekend's physical arm-wrestle with the All Blacks — full depth, full options, and one less selection headache for the coaching staff to manage before Saturday.

sections.heading: Wallabies yet to show their hand
sections.body: Australia have not confirmed their run-on side for Saturday, so the team news column stays blank on the eve of Round 2. What is known: home conditions and an extra day's recovery after their own Round 1 outing. Nothing has been given away out of the Wallabies camp this week, and France go in assuming a full-strength selection until proven otherwise.

sections.heading: Seventh and searching for a foothold
sections.body: France sit seventh of 12 after Round 1, the unbeaten record already gone following the 32-34 loss to New Zealand, points difference sitting at -2. A first win in Australia would settle both the log and the nerves in one move; another defeat would leave the campaign needing repair from Round 3 onward. The table is early, but positions won't stay this forgiving for long.

sections.heading: Backing the same shape
sections.body: No signals of a shake-up this week — the group that got over the line against Australia travels largely intact, camp mood settled rather than triumphant. Round two brings its own exam: back it up away from home, and Ireland stop being a one-week story. That is the whole assignment heading into Japan, nothing more dressed up than that.

sections.heading: Clean sheet, for now
sections.body: Nothing fresh has landed on the injury list since Thursday's session — no new withdrawals confirmed ahead of Saturday's Test in Japan. That is the quiet good news the medical staff will take rather than shout about, given how little separated Ireland and Australia last weekend. Watch the captain's run for any late change before kickoff.

sections.heading: Japan's team sheet, still a mystery
sections.body: Japan's side for Saturday had not been confirmed as of Friday, leaving the team sheet as the week's genuine unknown. What is certain is the setting — round two on home soil, in front of a crowd with every reason to lift the hosts. Ireland will get a clearer read once teams land on Friday evening.

sections.heading: Fifth, and unfinished business
sections.body: One from one leaves Ireland fifth of 12 heading into the weekend, +2 on points difference after squeezing past Australia 33-31. Nothing separates the chasing pack this early, which is exactly why round two matters more than the table suggests — win in Japan and a promising opener becomes form, lose and it becomes a wobble.

sections.heading: Answers still under wraps
sections.body: Italy's coaches have used this week to dissect a performance that leaked 27 points to Japan in the tournament opener. No changes have been confirmed publicly, and the group that finished round one remains the base picture until a team announcement lands. The real story is where fixes get made — up front, out wide, or in game management — all under scrutiny after a tough start.

sections.heading: No fresh news to report
sections.body: No injury news has been confirmed for Saturday's Test against New Zealand, and nothing has changed on that front since yesterday. The pool that started against Japan remains the presumptive base until a team announcement lands. Any fresh knocks from that outing haven't been made public, so the group should be treated as fit and available until told otherwise.

sections.heading: New Zealand: the step up
sections.body: New Zealand arrive as a considerably bigger physical and tactical examination than Japan, with their round one form the only reliable line of reference this week. No team news for the weekend had been confirmed as this went to print, so their tournament opener remains the strongest guide available. Line speed and set-piece precision look the likely difference-makers if that pattern holds.

sections.heading: Sitting 11th of 12
sections.body: Italy go into the weekend at 11th of 12, a product of the 10-27 opening defeat to Japan and a points difference of -17. A first win of the tournament would be a genuine foothold; another loss risks compounding the pool damage before the competition reaches its business end. The table remains provisional until the round's other fixtures are played.

sections.heading: No changes, no news — Townsend stays the course
sections.body: Round 1 delivered a shootout at 47-38, built on width and composure under pressure, and nothing suggests Gregor Townsend is tearing up that template for the trip south. No squad news has broken cover today, so read the quiet as backing continuity — same shape, same trust, one more away performance required to make it two from two.

sections.heading: Injury list still blank
sections.body: No new withdrawals have surfaced since yesterday, and nothing from camp points to changes in the group that started against Argentina. Saturday's Test will properly stress-test squad depth for the first time this campaign, but as things stand the injury list is exactly where it was 24 hours ago — blank.

sections.heading: South Africa's team sheet still to land
sections.body: No South Africa team sheet had landed by the time of writing, so Scotland prepare for the version everyone expects — heavy up front, direct off nine, comfortable grinding an arm wrestle at home. A raucous crowd and the usual selection depth up front are certainties; which names wear the jerseys is the only thing still to be confirmed.

sections.heading: Third of 12, ledger in good shape
sections.body: One win from one has Scotland third of 12 heading into the weekend, the 47-38 season opener leaving a healthy +9 on the differential column. Another result in South Africa nudges that position higher; even a bonus-point loss keeps the ledger ticking over nicely before the group table resets again next round.

sections.heading: Steady as she goes
sections.body: Wales opened the Nations Championship with intent, banking a 39-24 win over Fiji, and the build-up to Round 2 has carried the same tone. Nothing here smells of wholesale change — a group that clicked early gets the chance to show it wasn't a one-off, with Argentina up next to test whether that form travels on the road.

sections.heading: Clean bill through Thursday
sections.body: Nothing new has emerged out of camp since Thursday, and the squad that started against Fiji remains available heading into Saturday's Test in Argentina. No knocks, no late scares, no fitness question marks — about as clean a build-up as Wales could hope for going into a Round 2 away trip.

sections.heading: Pumas bite hardest at home
sections.body: Argentina go into Round 2 on home soil, and history says the Pumas are hardest to shift there — physical up front, patient with ball in hand, happy to grind a game down. Expect their forward pack to dominate the selection talk this week, with that battle shaping as the real test of Wales's opening-round form.

sections.heading: Second of 12, for now
sections.body: One round in, Wales sit second of 12, unbeaten, with a scoring difference of plus 15 after the Fiji win. Saturday's trip to face Argentina is the first proper gauge of where this squad stands away from home — win it, and second place becomes very hard for anyone else to argue with this weekend.

sections.heading: No fresh team news
sections.body: No squad news has broken out of the camp today, and that's arguably the story in itself: after a 38-47 opening loss to Scotland, this group's response matters more than who wears which number. Expect selection chatter to build once Wales confirm their own hand, but for now the message out of camp is about accountability, not alarm.

sections.heading: Quiet on the physio front
sections.body: Nothing new from the medical room since yesterday — no fresh withdrawals have been flagged ahead of Saturday's Test against Wales. That's the extent of the update: a quiet injury desk is still an update, and for a side already down 38-47 on the ledger, no bad news counts as good news heading into round two.

sections.heading: Wales' team still to come
sections.body: Wales hadn't named their run-on side by Friday evening, so the finer detail of Saturday's threat still waits. What's known: round two openers rarely forgive slow starts, and a Pumas pack still smarting from round one will want the collisions early rather than spend the weekend chasing the game from behind again.

sections.heading: Ninth of 12, for now
sections.body: As things stand Argentina sit ninth of 12 after one match, having lost 38-47 to Scotland for a points difference of -9. Beating Wales won't rewrite the log by itself, but it's the difference between building a case for the weeks ahead and going two defeats deep before round three even arrives.

sections.heading: Fixing the finish, not the personnel
sections.body: Round 1 answered a few questions and raised plenty more. A one-point defeat to Ireland leaves the group with plenty to fix before France arrive, and inside camp the conversation this week has centred on discipline and game management in the closing quarter rather than wholesale personnel change. Expect a settled group building on last week's shape, not a reactive shake-up.

sections.heading: Camp report: unchanged since Thursday
sections.body: Nothing fresh to flag since Thursday's session — the group that trained this week remains unchanged, and no withdrawals have been added to the injury column ahead of Saturday's Test against France. Official team news will confirm who is genuinely fit versus simply available, but for now camp reports as clean as it was 24 hours ago. A quiet update is still an update.

sections.heading: France's team sheet still to land
sections.body: France's team news for Saturday hadn't landed by the time of writing, so the exact French XV is this morning's open question — check the app once it drops. This is a side with a reputation for setting terms up front, and Saturday's sheet will show how much of that stays intact through a rotated bench. Worth another look before kickoff.

sections.heading: Friday's log: eighth of 12, all to play for
sections.body: As Friday's table stands, Australia sit eighth of 12 after the Round 1 stumble against Ireland — W0 D0 L1, 31 points for, 33 against, a net differential of -2. Beat France and that early ledger starts looking like a blip; lose again and the gap to the top of the log stretches further. Saturday carries more weight than a routine Round 2 fixture.

sections.heading: A quiet build after the Italy win
sections.body: No team news has broken today beyond what the log already shows — the group that saw off Italy 27-10 carries that shape and confidence into the build-up. Nothing suggests a shake-up is coming; if anything changes before Saturday it's likely to be about freshness rather than a rethink, with round one's template doing the talking for now.

sections.heading: Camp reports clean, for now
sections.body: Nothing new has surfaced since yesterday — no withdrawals have been confirmed ahead of Saturday's Test, and the squad that handled Italy remains the working group heading into the week's final training days. Any late change lands in the app's live team sheet rather than here; today's story, for once, is the absence of one.

sections.heading: Ireland's team news still to land
sections.body: Ireland's selection for Saturday hadn't been confirmed as of this morning, with the announcement expected in the countdown to kickoff — the app's team-sheet feed will carry it once it drops. Until then, this reads like a routine build-up week for the visitors, rather than one carrying fresh selection headaches into Japan.

sections.heading: One win in, and the log looks kind
sections.body: The Italy win has Japan sitting handy in the log, unbeaten after round one with a healthy points difference behind them. Beat Ireland and that log position only strengthens heading into the rest of the pool; slip up and it's straight back into the pack. Round two carries real weight either way.

sections.heading: Still building on the France win
sections.body: New Zealand haven't confirmed a matchday 23 for Italy yet, so today's line is about mood rather than paper changes. A nervy Round 1 win over France, 34-32, was the kind of start that keeps a camp honest rather than complacent. With little separating two well-matched sides in that opener, the story this week is tidying the edges, not reinventing anything.

sections.heading: Clean bill, for now
sections.body: Nothing new to add out of camp since yesterday, and no fresh names for the injury list ahead of Saturday's Test against Italy. That's a clean update two rounds into the tournament, which counts as good news at this stage. We'll keep checking as team news firms up through the week and flag anything that changes before kickoff.

sections.heading: Italy's XV: still to land
sections.body: Italy hadn't confirmed their run-on side for Saturday's Test at the time of writing, so the shape of the challenge is still to be nailed down. That team sheet, once released, will be the first real read on this opponent rather than assumption. New Zealand's planning stays the same either way: control the head-to-head collisions and don't let it turn scrappy.

sections.heading: Fourth of 12, for now
sections.body: New Zealand sit fourth of 12 off the back of a 34-32 win over France, a +2 points difference doing some early heavy lifting on the log. Beat Italy and it's two from two heading toward the business end of the pool stage. Slip up instead, and that tidy log position turns from convenience into pressure fast.

sections.heading: Backing what already works
sections.body: No changes have been flagged in the build-up to Scotland, and there's no reason to expect any: the pack that flattened England's set piece a week ago picked itself. Round one announced the depth chart is coming for whichever team lines up in green — round two is Scotland's turn to find out how deep it runs.

sections.heading: Camp reports clean
sections.body: No new injury concerns have surfaced out of the Bok camp this week. Everyone who started or came off the bench against England appears set to be available again, with no fresh knocks flagged ahead of Saturday's Test against Scotland. If that changes before kickoff, it will be the week's first genuine surprise.

sections.heading: Scotland's team sheet still to land
sections.body: Gregor Townsend's selection for Saturday hadn't been confirmed as we went to print, so the shape of Scotland's challenge is still filling in. What's certain: they need a cleaner, more clinical eighty minutes than most sides have managed against this pack lately, and a fast start will matter more than anything held back for the final quarter.

sections.heading: Two from two would be tidy
sections.body: South Africa head into the weekend top of the log on a healthy points difference after putting England away in round one. Win Saturday and the group phase starts to take care of itself; slip up and Scotland close the gap immediately. One result away from a settled few weeks, or a scramble.
